text: The surgeon discusses achieving the critical view of safety by clipping the cystic duct proximally and removing fiber fatty tissue to straighten the cystic duct. A balloon is used to help break through any valves within the duct.

The surgeon meticulously cleans the fat from the surface of the common bile duct to enable cannulation and avoid back walling the cystic duct. The cystic duct is cleaned completely down to the common bile duct, preparing the area for potential ductotomy extension if stones are larger than nine millimeters.

The surgeon demonstrates creating a ductotomy as high as possible on the cystic duct, allowing room for extension if needed due to larger stones. The ductotomy may be extended to the common bile duct, with some bleeding observed, which is controlled using heat.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to control bleeding while creating a ductotomy on the anterior lateral wall of the cystic duct. The ductotomy is extended to the anterior wall of the common bile duct for easier suturing. Stones are visible as the surgeon squeezes the cystic duct to evacuate them.

The surgeon manually squeezes the cystic duct to evacuate stones, aiming to clear as many stones as possible before performing a cholangiogram. As stones are removed, bile begins to appear, indicating successful clearance, and irrigation is initiated.

The surgeon creates another ductotomy without using heat to avoid bleeding. The ductotomy is extended on the anterior wall to reach the anterior wall of the common bile duct. A wire is used for manipulation, and clamps such as Kumar or Olsen may be used for performing a cholangiogram.

The surgeon prepares to flush the cystic duct using a cholangiogram catheter, which may be an Olsen, Kumar, or self-fixating balloon type. The catheter options include Finnelli or regular cholangiogram catheters in sizes four, five, or six French. Saline or water is used for flushing, while anesthesia administers one milligram of glucagon to the patient.

The surgeon increases pressure in the common bile duct, causing stones to emerge. A cholangiogram is performed, and a 150 cm soft wire is inserted followed by a soft balloon. The balloon is inflated to no more than five PSI, with part of it remaining outside the cystic duct, to dilate the duct and break its valves for cannulation and stone removal.

The surgeon dilates the cystic duct using a balloon, ensuring part of the balloon remains outside the duct to prevent dilation of the common bile duct. A colodoco scope is introduced, with pressurized water dripping through the scope to maintain controlled flow.

The surgeon attempts to dilate the cystic duct using a balloon, ensuring part of the balloon remains outside the duct. Struggling to advance the colodocuscope or ureteroscope, the surgeon decides to cut to address potential valves obstructing the passage. The dissection reveals the presence of valves, which the surgeon attempts to dilate.

The surgeon passes the colodocus scope through the cystic duct, encountering a complication with an injury to the posterior cystic duct wall. The injury is visible and addressed by placing a clip proximally and using an endo loop. The surgeon ensures pressurized water is dripping to maintain visualization while manipulating the common bile duct to remove a larger stone without extending the ductotomy.

The surgeon uses a wire and basket to retrieve stones from the bile duct. The basket is deployed and opened behind or next to the stone, then closed slowly to capture the stone. Stones larger than eight millimeters are not suitable for this procedure due to the balloon's dilation limit.

The surgeon advances the scope into the duodenum and begins a slow examination of the common bile duct, similar to a colonoscopy, pulling back gradually to ensure thorough inspection. The water flow is aggressive, affecting the bedside assistant, indicating the need for reduced pressure for optimal visualization. Depending on the cystic duct insertion, the surgeon attempts to flex the ureteroscope or colodoco scope proximally to view the right and left hepatic ducts, though this may be hindered by a septum between the common hepatic and cystic duct.

The surgeon carefully opens a basket behind an impacted stone in the cystic duct and slowly closes it to attempt removal. Due to the complete transection of the cystic duct, an arm is used to hold the structure. The impacted stone presents difficulty in passing the basket next to it, necessitating a lift maneuver.

The surgeon attempts to pass a basket behind an impacted stone in the cystic duct, aiming to stabilize the scope for the assistant to grab the stone. The stone is difficult to disimpact, and efforts to pass a wire or basket next to it are unsuccessful.

The surgeon performs laser lithotripsy to fragment an impacted stone in the common bile duct. The laser is used to break the stone into smaller pieces for removal, as passing a wire next to the stone was not possible.
